title: Quantization formats and their tradeoffs
description: How fp16, bf16, int8, and int4 trade memory and speed against numerical precision and quality.
body: Use when choosing a numeric format for training or inference. bf16 keeps fp32's exponent range with less mantissa, stabilizing training; fp16 risks overflow without loss scaling; int8 and int4 shrink memory sharply but need calibration and can degrade hard cases. Do not equate lower bits with free savings — quality loss is task-dependent and uneven. Actions: match format to stage (bf16 for training stability, int8/int4 for inference under a quality budget) and benchmark each. Validation signals include a precision-vs-quality benchmark and a chosen format inside the budget. Risk boundary: aggressive int4 can break long-context reasoning; if quality falls outside budget, escalate rather than accept silent degradation.
